subject: ap-chemistry
title: Methanoic Acid: Equilibrium, pH, Lewis Structure & Catalysis
stimulus: Methanoic acid (formic acid), HCOOH, ionizes in water: HCOOH(aq) ‚áĆ H‚Āļ(aq) + HCOO‚ĀĽ(aq), $K_a = 1.8 \times 10^{-4}$.

parts.prompt: Write the expression for the equilibrium constant, $K_a$, for the reaction.
parts.scoring_notes: The Ka expression places products over reactants, each raised to the power of its stoichiometric coefficient. Pure liquid water is omitted. Ka = [H‚Āļ][HCOO‚ĀĽ] / [HCOOH] = [H‚āÉO‚Āļ][HCOO‚ĀĽ] / [HCOOH].

scoring_points.description: For the correct Ka expression with products over reactants and water omitted: 1 point

alternatives.correct_example:
$K_a = \dfrac{[\text{H}^+][\text{HCOO}^-]}{[\text{HCOOH}]}$

Products (H‚Āļ and HCOO‚ĀĽ) in numerator; reactant HCOOH in denominator. Water is a pure liquid and is not included in the equilibrium expression.

wrong_examples: Including [H‚āāO] in the denominator ‚ÄĒ water is a pure liquid and is excluded from the Ka expression.
Writing Ka = [HCOOH]/[H‚Āļ][HCOO‚ĀĽ] ‚ÄĒ this is the inverse (1/Ka), not Ka.
common_traps: Ka = [products]/[reactants]. Omit pure liquids (H‚āāO) and pure solids. Each concentration is raised to its stoichiometric coefficient (all 1 here).

parts.prompt: Calculate the pH of a 0.25 M solution of HCOOH.
parts.scoring_notes: ICE table: HCOOH ‚áĆ H‚Āļ + HCOO‚ĀĽ. Initial: [HCOOH] = 0.25 M, [H‚Āļ] = [HCOO‚ĀĽ] = 0. Change: ‚ąíx, +x, +x. Equilibrium: Ka = x¬≤/(0.25 ‚ąí x) ‚Čą x¬≤/0.25 = 1.8√ó10‚ĀĽ‚Āī. x = ‚ąö(1.8√ó10‚ĀĽ‚Āī √ó 0.25) = ‚ąö(4.5√ó10‚ĀĽ‚ĀĶ) = 6.71√ó10‚ĀĽ¬≥ M. Check: x/0.25 = 2.7% < 5%, approximation valid. pH = ‚ąílog(6.71√ó10‚ĀĽ¬≥) ‚Čą 2.17.

scoring_points.description: For the correct calculated [H‚Āļ] using ICE table and Ka: 1 point

required_elements: ICE table setup: $K_a = x^2/(0.25 - x) \approx x^2/0.25 = 1.8 \times 10^{-4}$
alternatives.correct_example:
ICE table:
$\begin{array}{lccc} & \text{HCOOH} & \text{H}^+ & \text{HCOO}^- \\ I & 0.25 & 0 & 0 \\ C & -x & +x & +x \\ E & 0.25-x & x & x \end{array}$

$K_a = \dfrac{x^2}{0.25 - x} \approx \dfrac{x^2}{0.25} = 1.8 \times 10^{-4}$
$x^2 = 4.5 \times 10^{-5}$
$x = [\text{H}^+] = 6.71 \times 10^{-3}$ M

wrong_examples: Using 1.8√ó10‚ĀĽ‚Āī directly as the [H‚Āļ] ‚ÄĒ Ka is not the concentration; must solve the ICE table.
Setting up Ka = x/(0.25 ‚ąí x) instead of x¬≤/(0.25 ‚ąí x) ‚ÄĒ both H‚Āļ and HCOO‚ĀĽ each contribute a factor of x.
common_traps: Weak acid ICE: Ka = x¬≤/(C ‚ąí x). If x/C < 5%, approximate x¬≤/C. Here x/0.25 ‚Čą 2.7% ‚ÄĒ valid. Then solve for x = ‚ąö(Ka √ó C).

scoring_points.description: For the correct pH consistent with [H‚Āļ] from part b1: 1 point

alternatives.correct_example:
$\text{pH} = -\log(6.71 \times 10^{-3}) = 2.17$

(Accept pH consistent with student's [H‚Āļ] from part b1.)

wrong_examples: Using [H‚Āļ] = 0.25 M ‚Üí pH = 0.60 ‚ÄĒ this would only be correct for a strong acid.
common_traps: pH = ‚ąílog[H‚Āļ]. Use the [H‚Āļ] from the ICE table (not the initial concentration). Answer should be consistent with b1.

parts.prompt: In the box below, complete the Lewis electron-dot diagram for HCOOH. Show all bonding and nonbonding valence electrons.
parts.scoring_notes: HCOOH (formic acid) structure: H‚ąíO‚ąíC(=O)‚ąíH. Carbon is double-bonded to one oxygen and single-bonded to the ‚ąíOH oxygen and to H. The carbonyl oxygen has 2 lone pairs; the hydroxyl oxygen has 2 lone pairs. Total valence electrons = 1+6+4+6+1 = 18. All lone pairs and the C=O double bond must be shown.

parts.prompt:
In aqueous solution, the compound H‚āāNNH‚āā reacts according to the equation: H‚āāNNH‚āā(aq) + H‚āāO(l) ‚áĆ H‚āāNNH‚āÉ‚Āļ(aq) + OH‚ĀĽ(aq), Kb = 1.3 √ó 10‚ĀĽ‚Ā∂. A 50.0 mL sample of 0.25 M H‚āāNNH‚āā(aq) is combined with a 50.0 mL sample of 0.25 M HCOOH(aq).
(i) Write the balanced net ionic equation for the reaction that occurs when H‚āāNNH‚āā is combined with HCOOH.
(ii) Is the resulting solution acidic, basic, or neutral? Justify your answer.
parts.scoring_notes:
(i) Proton transfer: H‚āāNNH‚āā (base) accepts H‚Āļ from HCOOH (acid). Net ionic: H‚āāNNH‚āā(aq) + HCOOH(aq) ‚Üí H‚āāNNH‚āÉ‚Āļ(aq) + HCOO‚ĀĽ(aq). Equal moles of each reactant are present (0.0125 mol each), so reaction goes to completion producing equal moles of H‚āāNNH‚āÉ‚Āļ and HCOO‚ĀĽ.
(ii) Acidic. Ka(H‚āāNNH‚āÉ‚Āļ) = Kw/Kb(H‚āāNNH‚āā) = 1√ó10‚ĀĽ¬Ļ‚Āī / 1.3√ó10‚ĀĽ‚Ā∂ = 7.7√ó10‚ĀĽ‚ĀĻ. Ka(HCOO‚ĀĽ) = Kw/Ka(HCOOH) = 1√ó10‚ĀĽ¬Ļ‚Āī / 1.8√ó10‚ĀĽ‚Āī = 5.6√ó10‚ĀĽ¬Ļ¬Ļ. Since Ka(H‚āāNNH‚āÉ‚Āļ) > Ka(HCOO‚ĀĽ), H‚āāNNH‚āÉ‚Āļ is the stronger acid, so solution is acidic.

scoring_points.description: For the correct balanced net ionic equation (H‚āāNNH‚āā + HCOOH ‚Üí H‚āāNNH‚āÉ‚Āļ + HCOO‚ĀĽ): 1 point

alternatives.correct_example:
$\text{H}_2\text{NNH}_2(aq) + \text{HCOOH}(aq) \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{NNH}_3^+(aq) + \text{HCOO}^-(aq)$

H‚āāNNH‚āā acts as the base (accepts H‚Āļ); HCOOH acts as the acid (donates H‚Āļ). This is a proton-transfer (Br√łnsted‚ÄďLowry) neutralization.

wrong_examples: Including spectator ions (Na‚Āļ, K‚Āļ) ‚ÄĒ the net ionic equation shows only species that participate in the reaction.
Writing H‚āāNNH‚āā + HCOOH ‚Üí H‚āāNNH‚āā¬∑H‚Āļ + HCOO‚ĀĽ without proper formula for the conjugate acid H‚āāNNH‚āÉ‚Āļ.
common_traps: Identify the acid (HCOOH, donates H‚Āļ) and base (H‚āāNNH‚āā, accepts H‚Āļ). Net ionic: write the proton transfer directly without spectator ions. The conjugate acid is H‚āāNNH‚āÉ‚Āļ (protonated hydrazine).

scoring_points.description: For identifying the solution as acidic with a correct Ka comparison justification: 1 point

required_elements: $K_a(\text{H}_2\text{NNH}_3^+) = K_w/K_b = 7.7 \times 10^{-9}$ vs $K_a(\text{HCOO}^-) = K_w/K_a(\text{HCOOH}) = 5.6 \times 10^{-11}$; H‚āāNNH‚āÉ‚Āļ is stronger acid ‚Üí solution acidic.
alternatives.correct_example:
Acidic. After complete neutralization, equal amounts of H‚āāNNH‚āÉ‚Āļ and HCOO‚ĀĽ are produced.
$K_a(\text{H}_2\text{NNH}_3^+) = \dfrac{K_w}{K_b(\text{H}_2\text{NNH}_2)} = \dfrac{1.0 \times 10^{-14}}{1.3 \times 10^{-6}} = 7.7 \times 10^{-9}$
$K_a(\text{HCOO}^-) = \dfrac{K_w}{K_a(\text{HCOOH})} = \dfrac{1.0 \times 10^{-14}}{1.8 \times 10^{-4}} = 5.6 \times 10^{-11}$
Since Ka(H‚āāNNH‚āÉ‚Āļ) > Ka(HCOO‚ĀĽ), the cation is a stronger acid than the anion is a base ‚Üí solution is acidic.

wrong_examples: Saying neutral ‚ÄĒ equal moles of acid and base reacted does NOT mean neutral if the conjugate ions have different Ka/Kb values.
Saying basic ‚ÄĒ H‚āāNNH‚āÉ‚Āļ has a larger Ka (7.7√ó10‚ĀĽ‚ĀĻ) than the Ka of HCOO‚ĀĽ (5.6√ó10‚ĀĽ¬Ļ¬Ļ), making the solution net acidic.
common_traps: When a weak acid reacts with a weak base in equimolar amounts, the resulting solution pH depends on the relative Ka of the conjugate acid vs. Ka of the conjugate base. Ka(conjugate acid) = Kw/Kb(parent base); Ka(conjugate base) = Kw/Ka(parent acid). The larger Ka wins.

parts.prompt: When a catalyst is added to a solution of HCOOH(aq), the reaction HCOOH(aq) ‚Üí H‚āā(g) + CO‚āā(g) occurs. Is the reaction a redox reaction? Justify your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: Yes, this is a redox reaction. In HCOOH: H bonded to O is +1 (decreases in H‚āā to 0); C has oxidation state +2 (HCOOH: H=+1, O=‚ąí2 each, so +1 + C + (‚ąí2) + (‚ąí2) + 1 = 0 ‚Üí C = +2). In CO‚āā: C = +4. H goes from +1 to 0 (reduced); C goes from +2 to +4 (oxidized). Accept either change as justification.

scoring_points.description: For 'Yes' with a correct oxidation state change justification: 1 point

required_elements: Yes, the reaction is a redox reaction.
Either: H changes from +1 (in HCOOH) to 0 (in H‚āā), OR C changes from +2 (in HCOOH) to +4 (in CO‚āā).
alternatives.correct_example: Yes, this is a redox reaction. In HCOOH, carbon has an oxidation state of +2 (calculated: +1 + C + 2(‚ąí2) + 1 = 0 ‚Üí C = +2). In CO‚āā, carbon has an oxidation state of +4. Carbon is oxidized (+2 ‚Üí +4). Additionally, hydrogen changes from +1 in HCOOH to 0 in H‚āā (reduced). Since oxidation states change, the reaction is a redox reaction.

wrong_examples: Saying no ‚ÄĒ oxidation states do change in this reaction; H goes from +1 to 0 and C goes from +2 to +4.
Citing only that hydrogen is produced ‚ÄĒ must cite an actual change in oxidation number.
common_traps: Assign oxidation states in HCOOH: H(O-bonded) = +1, H(C-bonded) = +1, O = ‚ąí2 each. Solve for C: +1 + C + 2(‚ąí2) + 1 = 0 ‚Üí C = +2. In CO‚āā: C = +4. Redox = any oxidation number change.

parts.prompt: The reaction occurs in a rigid 4.3 L vessel at 25¬įC, and the total pressure is monitored, as shown in the graph above. The vessel originally did not contain any gas. Calculate the number of moles of CO‚āā(g) produced in the reaction. (Assume that the amount of CO‚āā(g) dissolved in the solution is negligible.)
parts.scoring_notes: From the graph, total pressure at equilibrium ‚Čą 0.24 atm. Reaction: HCOOH(aq) ‚Üí H‚āā(g) + CO‚āā(g). Equal moles of H‚āā and CO‚āā are produced (1:1 ratio), so P(CO‚āā) = P(H‚āā) = 0.24/2 = 0.12 atm. Use ideal gas law: n(CO‚āā) = PV/RT = (0.12 atm)(4.3 L) / [(0.08206 L¬∑atm/mol¬∑K)(298 K)] = 0.516/24.45 ‚Čą 0.021 mol.

scoring_points.description: For the correct partial pressure of CO‚āā using stoichiometry (half total pressure): 1 point

alternatives.correct_example:
From the graph, total pressure = 0.24 atm. Since HCOOH ‚Üí H‚āā + CO‚āā produces equal moles of each gas:
$P_{\text{CO}_2} = P_{\text{H}_2} = P_{\text{total}}/2 = 0.24/2 = 0.12$ atm

wrong_examples: Using the total pressure as P(CO‚āā) ‚ÄĒ the total pressure equals P(H‚āā) + P(CO‚āā), and both gases are produced in equal amounts.
Reading the wrong value from the graph ‚ÄĒ use the final plateau pressure, not an intermediate value.
common_traps: Two gases are produced in a 1:1 ratio (HCOOH ‚Üí H‚āā + CO‚āā). So P(CO‚āā) = P(H‚āā) = P(total)/2. Read the final equilibrium pressure from the graph plateau.

scoring_points.description: For the correct moles of CO‚āā using PV = nRT: 1 point

alternatives.correct_example: $n(\text{CO}_2) = \dfrac{P_{\text{CO}_2} \cdot V}{RT} = \dfrac{(0.12 \text{ atm})(4.3 \text{ L})}{(0.08206 \text{ L¬∑atm/mol¬∑K})(298 \text{ K})} = \dfrac{0.516}{24.45} \approx 0.021 \text{ mol}$

wrong_examples: Using total pressure 0.24 atm instead of P(CO‚āā) = 0.12 atm ‚ÄĒ must account for equal moles of H‚āā also present.
Not converting ‚ÄĒ temperature must be in Kelvin (25¬įC = 298 K).
common_traps: PV = nRT: n = PV/RT. Use P in atm, V in L, T in K. Accept student's pressure from f1 (follow-through).

parts.prompt: After the reaction has proceeded for several minutes, does the amount of catalyst increase, decrease, or remain the same? Justify your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: The amount of catalyst remains the same. A catalyst speeds up a reaction by providing an alternative lower-activation-energy pathway, but is not consumed in the overall reaction. It is regenerated at the end of the catalytic cycle, so the amount present before and after the reaction is unchanged.

scoring_points.description: For 'remains the same' with a correct catalysis justification: 1 point

required_elements: The amount of catalyst remains the same.
A catalyst is not consumed in the reaction ‚ÄĒ it is regenerated and provides only an alternative lower-activation-energy pathway.
alternatives.correct_example: The amount of catalyst remains the same. By definition, a catalyst participates in the mechanism but is regenerated by the end of the reaction. It lowers the activation energy and increases the reaction rate but does not appear in the overall balanced equation as a reactant or product.

wrong_examples: Saying the catalyst decreases ‚ÄĒ catalysts are not consumed; they are regenerated.
Saying the catalyst increases ‚ÄĒ catalysts are neither produced nor consumed; they end up in the same amount as when the reaction started.
common_traps: Catalyst definition: speeds up reaction by providing lower Ea pathway; is NOT consumed. Net change in catalyst amount = 0. Do not confuse catalyst with reactant.
